title: Chapter 10 - Accounting for Borrowing Costs - LKAS 23
description: A challenging assessment of conceptual understanding and application of LKAS 23 principles, including capitalization, cessation, suspension, and eligible borrowing cost calculation.
subject: ACC1370 - Financial Accounting and Reporting

questions.question: An entity borrows funds generally and uses them to construct a qualifying asset. If the entity has three outstanding general borrowings with rates of 8%, 10%, and 12%, which interest rate should be used as the capitalization rate?
options: The highest rate (12%) to be conservative.
The lowest rate (8%) to maximize profit.
A simple arithmetic average of the three rates (10%).
The weighted average of the borrowing costs applicable to all outstanding general borrowings.
questions.answer: The weighted average of the borrowing costs applicable to all outstanding general borrowings.
questions.rationale: For general borrowings, the capitalization rate must be the weighted average of the borrowing costs applicable to all general borrowings outstanding during the period. Simple average or individual rates are incorrect.

questions.question: Which scenario necessitates the **suspension** of borrowing cost capitalization under LKAS 23?
options: A temporary delay in construction due to high water levels that are common in the geographical region.
An extended period during which the entity suspends active development due to a civil unrest incident.
A period where substantial technical and administrative work is being carried out.
Minor modifications being made to a property to the purchaser's specific requirements after physical construction is complete.
questions.answer: An extended period during which the entity suspends active development due to a civil unrest incident.
questions.rationale: Capitalization is suspended during extended periods in which active development is suspended, as these are costs of holding partially completed assets. Delays that are a necessary part of the process or periods of active administrative work do not warrant suspension. Cessation occurs after substantially all activities are complete.

questions.question: A company is constructing a specialized manufacturing facility that takes three years to complete. The core principle of LKAS 23 mandates that the borrowing costs directly attributable to this construction should be:
options: Recognized immediately as an expense in the period incurred.
Capitalized as part of the cost of the asset.
Included as a deduction from revenue in the income statement.
Disclosed in the notes but not recognized in the financial statements until the asset is ready for use.
questions.answer: Capitalized as part of the cost of the asset.
questions.rationale: The core principle of LKAS 23 is that borrowing costs directly attributable to the acquisition, construction, or production of a qualifying asset form part of the cost of that asset.

questions.question: A qualifying asset is measured at fair value, such as a biological asset under LKAS 41. How should the borrowing costs related to the production of this asset be treated according to LKAS 23?
options: They must be capitalized, as it is a qualifying asset.
They are explicitly scoped out and are not required to be capitalized under LKAS 23.
They should be expensed immediately, regardless of the fair value measurement.
They should be disclosed in the notes but have no effect on the asset's carrying amount.
questions.answer: They are explicitly scoped out and are not required to be capitalized under LKAS 23.
questions.rationale: LKAS 23 does not require an entity to apply the Standard to borrowing costs directly attributable to the acquisition, construction, or production of a qualifying asset measured at fair value, such as a biological asset within the scope of LKAS 41.

questions.question: In the case of a specific borrowing for a qualifying asset, the amount of borrowing costs eligible for capitalization is the actual borrowing costs incurred **less**:
options: Any progress payments received from the customer.
Any investment income on the temporary investment of those specific borrowings.
The estimated exchange differences related to the borrowing.
The capitalized borrowing costs from the previous period.
questions.answer: Any investment income on the temporary investment of those specific borrowings.
questions.rationale: For specific borrowings, the amount eligible for capitalization is the actual costs incurred less any investment income earned on the temporary investment of those borrowed funds, as these funds are often invested pending their expenditure.

questions.question: Which of the following would NOT be considered a 'qualifying asset' under LKAS 23?
options: An intangible asset that takes four years to develop to be ready for its intended use.
Inventory of a wholesaler that will be sold without changing its original condition.
A power generation facility taking three years to construct.
A bearer plant taking a substantial period of time to reach maturity.
questions.answer: Inventory of a wholesaler that will be sold without changing its original condition.
questions.rationale: A qualifying asset is one that necessarily takes a substantial period of time to get ready for its intended use or sale. Inventories that are manufactured or produced over a short period of time, or assets ready for use/sale when acquired (like wholesale inventory), are not qualifying assets.

questions.question: For a qualifying asset that is constructed in usable parts (e.g., a business park with several separate buildings), when should the entity cease capitalizing borrowing costs?
options: When the final part of the entire asset is complete.
When substantially all activities necessary to prepare a completed individual part for its intended use or sale are complete.
When the total expenditures reach the amount of the specific borrowing.
Only when the entity starts using the first part of the asset.
questions.answer: When substantially all activities necessary to prepare a completed individual part for its intended use or sale are complete.
questions.rationale: When a qualifying asset is completed in parts and each part is capable of being used individually, capitalization should cease for a specific part when it is substantially ready for its intended use or sale, even if construction continues on other parts.

questions.question: The amount of borrowing costs an entity capitalizes during a period using the general borrowings method **must not exceed** what amount?
options: The total general borrowings outstanding during the period.
The total expenditure incurred on the qualifying asset during the period.
The total borrowing costs the entity incurred during that period.
The weighted average of the borrowing costs for all general borrowings.
questions.answer: The total borrowing costs the entity incurred during that period.
questions.rationale: A key limitation of the general borrowings method is that the amount of borrowing costs capitalized during a period cannot exceed the total amount of borrowing costs the entity incurred during that same period.

questions.question: When an entity applies LKAS 29 Financial Reporting in Hyperinflationary Economies, how is the part of borrowing costs that compensates for inflation treated?
options: It is fully capitalised as part of the asset cost.
It is deducted from the investment income of specific borrowings.
It is recognised as an expense in the period.
It is deferred and amortised over the asset's useful life.
questions.answer: It is recognised as an expense in the period.
questions.rationale: When applying LKAS 29, the part of borrowing costs that compensates for inflation is recognised as an expense in the period, in accordance with paragraph 21 of that Standard.

questions.question: Which of the following is included in the definition of 'Expenditures on a qualifying asset' for the purpose of commencing capitalization?
options: Future contractual commitments.
The assumption of interest-bearing liabilities.
The fair value of the asset upon completion.
The budgeted cost of construction.
questions.rationale: Expenditures on a qualifying asset include only those expenditures that have resulted in payments of cash, transfers of other assets, or the assumption of interest-bearing liabilities.

questions.question: An entity incurs borrowing costs and expenditures on an asset, but the development involves a six-month delay while it waits for a necessary governmental permit (a technical and administrative activity). How should the borrowing costs during this six-month period be treated?
options: Capitalization must be suspended because physical construction has paused.
The costs must be expensed as the asset is not in use.
Capitalization continues, as this is a necessary technical or administrative work to prepare the asset.
Only the portion related to the permit fee is capitalized; the interest cost is expensed.
questions.answer: Capitalization continues, as this is a necessary technical or administrative work to prepare the asset.
questions.rationale: The activities necessary to prepare the asset include technical and administrative work prior to physical construction. Capitalization does not normally suspend when the entity carries out substantial technical and administrative work.

questions.question: Which item is explicitly listed as a component that 'Borrowing costs may include' under LKAS 23?
options: Dividends declared on equity instruments.
Actual or imputed cost of equity.
Interest expense calculated using the effective interest method as described in SLFRS 9.
Revaluation surplus on investment properties.
questions.answer: Interest expense calculated using the effective interest method as described in SLFRS 9.
questions.rationale: Borrowing costs include interest expense calculated using the effective interest method as described in SLFRS 9, interest on lease liabilities, and certain exchange differences.

questions.question: An entity finishes the physical construction of a manufacturing plant. Only minor modifications, such as the final painting and installation of minor security equipment, remain. When should the entity cease capitalization of borrowing costs?
options: When the plant is physically ready for its intended use or sale, which is generally when the physical construction is complete.
Only after the final painting and security equipment are fully installed and operational.
When the first production run starts.
When the associated specific borrowing is repaid.
questions.answer: When the plant is physically ready for its intended use or sale, which is generally when the physical construction is complete.
questions.rationale: Capitalization ceases when substantially all the activities necessary to prepare the qualifying asset for its intended use or sale are complete. The asset is normally ready when physical construction is complete, even if minor modifications or routine administrative work remain.

questions.question: In determining the amount of borrowing costs directly attributable to a qualifying asset, the entity is seeking the borrowing costs that:
options: Are the lowest possible to minimize the asset cost.
Would have been avoided if the expenditure on the qualifying asset had not been made.
Are applicable to all debt instruments of the entity, regardless of purpose.
Are included in the lease liability interest component only.
questions.answer: Would have been avoided if the expenditure on the qualifying asset had not been made.
questions.rationale: The borrowing costs that are directly attributable are those that would have been avoided if the expenditure on the qualifying asset had not been made.

questions.question: If a qualifying asset's carrying amount (including capitalized borrowing costs) is found to exceed its recoverable amount, what action is required under LKAS 23?
options: The excess is immediately expensed as borrowing costs.
The borrowing costs previously capitalized must be reversed.
The carrying amount is written down or written off in accordance with the requirements of other relevant Standards.
Capitalization must cease immediately, even if the asset is incomplete.
questions.answer: The carrying amount is written down or written off in accordance with the requirements of other relevant Standards.
questions.rationale: When the carrying amount of a qualifying asset exceeds its recoverable amount or net realisable value, the carrying amount is written down or written off in accordance with the requirements of other Standards (e.g., LKAS 36 Impairment of Assets).

questions.question: The commencement date for capitalization is the date when the entity first meets all three conditions: incurring expenditures for the asset, incurring borrowing costs, and undertaking [dropdown_1] that are necessary to prepare the asset for its [dropdown_2].

options: physical construction
activities
payments
negotiations
intended use or sale
inspection by regulators
first production run
completion of accounting records

questions.rationale: The third condition for commencement is undertaking 'activities' that are necessary to prepare the asset for its 'intended use or sale'. Activities encompass more than just physical construction.

questions.question: Financial assets and inventories produced over a [dropdown_1] period of time are explicitly mentioned as assets that are [dropdown_2] qualifying assets under LKAS 23.

options: substantial
long
short
indefinite
always
never
sometimes
not

questions.rationale: Financial assets, and inventories manufactured over a 'short' period of time, are specifically excluded and are 'not' qualifying assets.

questions.question: Other borrowing costs that are not directly attributable to a qualifying asset must be recognized as an [dropdown_1] in the period in which they are [dropdown_2].

options: asset
equity item
expense
deferred cost
paid
contracted
incurred
settled

questions.rationale: All non-capitalizable borrowing costs must be recognised as an 'expense' in the period in which they are 'incurred'.

questions.question: The capitalization rate for general borrowings must be the weighted average of the borrowing costs applicable to all general borrowings of the entity that are ________ during the period.
questions.rationale: The capitalisation rate is the weighted average of borrowing costs applicable to all borrowings that are 'outstanding' during the period, excluding those specifically made for a qualifying asset that is complete.

questions.question: A qualifying asset is an asset that necessarily takes a ________ period of time to get ready for its intended use or sale.
questions.rationale: This is the key definition of a 'qualifying asset' as per LKAS 23.

questions.question: Borrowing costs that are directly attributable to a qualifying asset are capitalized as part of the cost of that asset when it is probable that they will result in future economic benefits and the costs can be measured ________.
questions.rationale: Capitalization requires meeting the general recognition criteria that it is probable they will result in future economic benefits and the costs can be measured 'reliably'.

questions.question: Which of the following conditions must **all** be met to commence the capitalization of borrowing costs?
options: Incurrence of borrowing costs.
Physical construction of the asset has begun.
The asset is legally ready for its intended use or sale.
Incurrence of expenditures for the asset.
Undertaking activities that are necessary to prepare the asset for its intended use or sale.
answer: Incurrence of expenditures for the asset.
Undertaking activities that are necessary to prepare the asset for its intended use or sale.
questions.rationale: Commencement requires three conditions: (a) expenditure incurred, (b) borrowing costs incurred, and (c) necessary activities undertaken. Physical construction starting is not strictly required, as activities include technical/administrative work.

questions.question: Which items are explicitly excluded from the scope of LKAS 23?
options: Inventories manufactured in large quantities on a repetitive basis.
Investment properties taking a long time to construct.
The actual or imputed cost of equity, including preferred capital not classified as a liability.
Assets that are ready for their intended use or sale when acquired.
Qualifying assets measured at fair value.
answer: Inventories manufactured in large quantities on a repetitive basis.
The actual or imputed cost of equity, including preferred capital not classified as a liability.
Qualifying assets measured at fair value.
questions.rationale: LKAS 23 does not deal with the cost of equity. It is also not required to be applied to inventories produced in large quantities on a repetitive basis or qualifying assets measured at fair value.

questions.question: In which circumstances should an entity NOT suspend the capitalization of borrowing costs?
options: When there is an extended period of inactivity due to market demand issues.
When the construction is temporarily delayed by high water levels that are a common part of the construction period in the region.
When the entity carries out substantial technical and administrative work during the construction period.
When land acquired for building purposes is held without any associated development activity.
answer: When the construction is temporarily delayed by high water levels that are a common part of the construction period in the region.
When the entity carries out substantial technical and administrative work during the construction period.
questions.rationale: Capitalization is not suspended when: (a) a temporary delay is a necessary part of the process, or (b) substantial technical and administrative work is being carried out. Inactivity or holding an asset without development causes suspension.

questions.question: Match the type of borrowing with the primary method for calculating eligible borrowing costs.
Match the type of cost with its treatment under LKAS 23.

matchPairs.left: Exchange differences regarded as interest adjustment
Cost of equity/preferred capital not a liability

questions.question: Match the capitalization period trigger with the relevant point in the asset's construction cycle.

matchPairs.right: Incurring all 3 trigger conditions (Expenditure, Borrowing Costs, Activities)
Substantially all activities to prepare for use/sale are complete

questions.question: A construction company has two outstanding general loans (A and B) and takes out a third, specific loan (C) for a new qualifying asset. Explain how the company should calculate the capitalization rate for the new asset's expenditures, particularly addressing whether all three loans should be included in the weighted average calculation.

A steel mill, which involves several processes carried out in sequence at different parts of the same site, is a qualifying asset. Contrast the cessation of borrowing cost capitalization for this steel mill with that of a business park comprising several independently usable buildings, justifying the difference in treatment under LKAS 23.

Discuss the three necessary conditions required for the **commencement** of borrowing cost capitalization. Furthermore, explain why 'activities necessary to prepare the asset for its intended use or sale' includes more than just the physical construction of the asset, providing one example.
